🌍 Endangered Species Explorer

Wild & Endangered

Meet three incredible species fighting for survival — tap below to explore!

CR — Critically Endangered

Amur Leopard

Panthera pardus orientalis

The world's rarest leopard — fewer than 100 adults remain in the wild. This ghostlike big cat survives in the harsh, forested landscape of the Russian Far East and northeastern China.

Rarest Cat
📏
Length
100–136 cm
⚖️
Weight
25–48 kg
⏳
Lifespan
10–15 yrs
🗺️
Home Range
15–30 km²
🐾
Cubs / Litter
1–4 cubs
🍖
Eats / Day
~10–12 kg
🍽️ Stealth Hunter Diet
🦌 Siberian Roe Deer90%
🐗 Wild Boar5%
🐭 Small Mammals & Birds5%
✨ Special Traits
❄️
Thick Winter Coat
Dense fur survives temps as low as −40°C, with a long bushy tail for insulation.
👁️
Exceptional Vision
Reflective eye layer boosts night hunting in low light.
🐾
Powerful Build
Can leap up to 6 meters in a single bound.
⚠️ Threats
Poaching
Killed for its beautiful coat and body parts used in traditional medicine.
Habitat Loss
Logging, fires, and road development fragment and degrade forest.
Prey Decline
Overhunting of natural prey reduces food availability.
Inbreeding Risk
Tiny population increases vulnerability to disease and genetic loss.
📍 Where It Lives

Temperate forests of the Russian Far East and a tiny border area of northeastern China.

🌲 Habitat: Mixed conifer-broadleaf forests, rugged terrain, deep valleys.
⛰️ Elevation: 300–1,200 m
🌡️ Climate: Cold, harsh winters down to −40°C
🟠 Historic: Wider Russia & China 🟢 Current: Tiny border zone
🛡️ Conservation Efforts

With fewer than 100 adults remaining, every leopard counts.

🌲Protected Areas
National parks & wildlife reserves
📷Camera Traps
10,000+ images for monitoring
👮Anti-Poaching
Patrols & rapid response
🤝Community
Local engagement & education

CR — Critically Endangered

Atlantic Sturgeon

Acipenser oxyrinchus oxyrinchus

A living fossil that's roamed Earth's waters for over 100 million years. This ancient giant migrates between ocean and freshwater rivers — and now faces extinction.

Ancient Giant
📏
Length
Up to 3.5 m
⚖️
Weight
Up to 360 kg
⏳
Lifespan
60–100+ yrs
🐣
Eggs / Spawn
200k–1M
🔞
Maturity
10–22 yrs
🗺️
Origin
Atlantic Coast
🍽️ Bottom Feeder Diet

Uses barbels and extensible mouth to vacuum food from riverbeds.

🦐 Crustaceans40%
🐚 Mollusks30%
🪱 Insects & Larvae20%
🐠 Small Fish10%
✨ Special Traits
🛡️
Armored Protection
Five rows of bony plates (scutes) provide protection and reduce drag.
🐛
Barbels
Four sensory barbels detect food hidden in riverbed sediment.
⚡
Electrosensitivity
Specialized cells detect electric fields from prey hidden in sediment.
🔄 Life Cycle
🌊
Spawning
Returns to freshwater rivers in spring
🥚
Eggs
Up to 1 million eggs over gravel beds
🐛
Larvae
Drift downstream toward estuaries
🐟
Juveniles
Grow in estuaries for several years
🌐
Adults
Enter the ocean to feed & mature
⚠️ Threats
Overfishing
Targeted for meat, roe (caviar), and caught as bycatch.
Habitat Loss
Dams block migration; river degradation affects spawning success.
Pollution
Chemicals and sediment harm eggs and larvae.
Climate Change
Rising temps and sea level changes impact habitats and food sources.
📍 Where It Lives

Native to the Atlantic coast of North America. Migrates between ocean and freshwater rivers.

🏔️ Juveniles: Estuaries and coastal inlets
🌊 Adults (feeding): Coastal waters and deep ocean
🏞️ Adults (spawning): Clean, free-flowing rivers with gravel beds
🟦 Historic: Full Atlantic Coast 🟡 Current: Fragmented stretches
🛡️ A Future Worth Fighting For

Conservation, river restoration, and sustainable fisheries are critical for survival.

🌊Protect Habitat
Restore rivers & fish passage
🎣Sustainable Fishing
Reduce bycatch & illegal fishing
🔬Support Research
Science-guided recovery
📢Spread Awareness
Every voice helps protect our waters

EN — Endangered

California Condor

Gymnogyps californianus

Once nearly extinct with just 27 birds in the wild, this symbol of resilience has been brought back from the brink through science, cooperation, and dedication. Now 500+ exist!

Comeback Bird
🪽
Wingspan
Up to 3.2 m
⚖️
Weight
9–14 kg
⏳
Lifespan
40–60+ yrs
🔞
Maturity
8–10 yrs
🥚
Eggs / Yr
1 every 1–2 yrs
💨
Daily Range
Up to 250 km
🎉 Conservation Success Story!
1987
27
birds in the wild
→
2024
500+
birds today!
Intensive conservation, captive breeding, reintroduction, and habitat protection have given this species a second chance. 🎊
🍽️ Nature's Cleanup Crew

Carrion makes up almost 100% of their diet — removing dead animals prevents disease spread.

🦌 Large Animal Carcasses80%
🐑 Medium Animals15%
🐇 Small Animals5%
✨ Special Traits
🌡️
Bare Head
Stays clean while feeding on carcasses with built-in heat regulation.
👃
Exceptional Smell
Can detect a carcass from over 5 km (≈3 miles) away.
🌬️
Soars for Miles
Uses thermal updrafts for hours with minimal wingbeats — up to 250 km/day!
🧪
Strong Stomach Acid
Safely eats bacteria-rich carrion that would sicken other birds.
🔄 Life Cycle
🥚
1 Egg
Laid every 1–2 years
🐣
Chick
Hatches after ~61–65 days; fledges at ~6 months
🦅
Juvenile
Dependent on parents for up to 1 year
🌟
Adult
Breeds at 8–10 years; mates for life
⚠️ Threats
Lead Poisoning
Ingesting lead from bullets in animal carcasses — top killer.
Habitat Loss
Development, mining, and human disturbance destroy nesting sites.
Power Lines & Collisions
Collisions with power lines and wind turbines are deadly.
Slow Reproduction
Only 1 egg every 1–2 years makes recovery very slow.
📍 Where It Lives

Native to western United States and northern Mexico — soaring over mountains, canyons, and coastal ranges.

🏔️ Habitat: Open landscapes, rugged canyons, coastal areas
🪺 Nesting: Cliff sites with reliable carrion nearby
⛰️ Elevation: Sea level to 2,700 m (9,000 ft)
🟣 Historic: Across North America 🟢 Current: Western US & Mexico
💚 How You Can Help
💰Support Conservation
Donate to Peregrine Fund & partners
🔫Reduce Lead
Support non-lead ammunition
🔭Respect Wildlife
Never disturb nesting or roosting
📣Spread Awareness
Share & inspire others to protect
